attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the labour ward at 24 weeks gestation with vaginal fluid loss and regular uterine tightening for two hours. She had an emergency cervical cerclage placed one week ago for cervical shortening. She received betamethasone injections three days ago and completed 48 hours of prophylactic antibiotics with the cerclage placement. She has no significant medical history and takes pregnancy multivitamins only.

On examination, she appears mildly unwell. Her pulse rate is 102/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.8°C. Her abdomen is soft with a gravid uterus and regular contractions every five minutes. Bedside ultrasound confirms cephalic presentation. Sterile speculum examination confirms rupture of membranes with pink/yellow fluid and cervix dilated to 3 cm. Cardiotocography shows regular uterine activity with normal fetal heart rate pattern. Investigations reveal white cell count 14 x 10^9/L (4-11) and C-reactive protein 25 mg/L (<5).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Continue cerclage and start intravenous antibiotics and magnesium sulphate
Continue cerclage and commence tocolysis and magnesium sulphate
Remove cerclage, start magnesium sulphate and intravenous antibiotics
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with preterm prelabour rupture of membranes (PPROM) at 24 weeks gestation with signs of early infection (fever, raised inflammatory markers) and regular uterine activity. The presence of a cervical cerclage in this setting requires immediate decision-making regarding its removal. Unfortunately it is highly likely this patient will go on to deliver her baby at extreme prematurity. If labour progresses with a cerclage in place it is likely to cause lacerations and tearing to the cervix.

Remove cerclage, start magnesium sulphate and intravenous antibiotics is the most appropriate management plan. The cerclage must be removed due to increased risk of ascending infection and potential cervical trauma if labour progresses. Magnesium sulphate provides fetal neuroprotection when delivery is anticipated before 30 weeks. Broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics are essential to treat potential chorioamnionitis and improve neonatal outcomes.

Continue cerclage and commence tocolysis would be inappropriate as it could worsen infection and delay necessary delivery in the setting of chorioamnionitis. Tocolysis is contraindicated when infection is present.

Emergency caesarean section is not indicated as there is no fetal compromise on cardiotocography, and vaginal delivery would be preferred at this extreme preterm gestation unless there are specific obstetric indications.

Continue cerclage and start intravenous antibiotics would be inadequate as the cerclage needs removal to prevent worsening infection and potential cervical trauma.

Remove cerclage and observe would be insufficient as it fails to provide necessary interventions including magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection and antibiotics for infection prevention.
learningPoints: Cervical cerclage should be removed if preterm prelabour rupture of membranes occurs due to increased risk of infection.
Magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection should be administered when delivery is anticipated before 30 weeks gestation.
Broad-spectrum antibiotics are indicated in preterm prelabour rupture of membranes to reduce risk of chorioamnionitis and improve neonatal outcomes.
references: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG). Measurement of cervical length for prediction of preterm birth [Internet]. Melbourne: RANZCOG; 2023 [cited 2024 June 7]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/Measurement-Cervical-Length-Preterm-Birth.pdf

Chatzakis C, Efthymiou A, Sotiriadis A, Makrydimas G. Emergency cerclage in singleton pregnancies with painless cervical dilatation: A meta-analysis. Acta Obstet Gynecol Scand. 2020 Nov;99(11):1444-1457. doi: 10.1111/aogs.13968. Epub 2020 Sep 16. PMID: 32757297.
National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). Preterm labour and birth [Internet]. London: NICE; 2015 [cited 2024 June 7]. Available from: https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng25

Queensland Health. Guideline for premature rupture of membranes at term [Internet]. Brisbane: Queensland Health; 2021 [cited 2024 June 7]. Available from: https://www.health.qld.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0019/140149/g-ptl.pdf
